en.extract:
The Cocos finch (Pinaroloxias inornata) or Cocos Island finch, is the only one of Darwin's finches not native to the Galápagos Islands, and the only member of the genus Pinaroloxias. Sometimes classified in the family Emberizidae, more recent studies have shown it to belong in the tanager family, Thraupidae. It is endemic to Cocos Island, a Pacific island which is approximately 360 miles (580 km) south of Costa Rica.


Taxonomy
The Cocos finch was formally described in 1843 by the English ornithologist John Gould under the binomial name Cactornis inornatus. The species was moved to a new genus Pinaroloxias by Richard Bowdler Sharpe in 1885. The genus name combines the Ancient Greek pinaros meaning "dirty" or "squalid" with Loxia, a genus introduced by Carl Linnaeus in 1758 for the crossbills. The specific epithet inornata is Latin for "plain" or "unadorned". The Cocos finch is a member of a group collectively known as Darwin's finches. Although traditionally placed with the buntings and New World sparrows in the family Emberizidae, molecular phylogenetic studies have shown that Darwin's finches are members of the subfamily Coerebinae within the tanager family Thraupidae. The Cocos finch was first categorized to be closer to the warbler finch clade due to the similar morphology of cousin warbler finches.
The Cocos finch shares many morphological similarities to other Darwin's finches despite having been separated from the Galapagos Islands. What is unique, however, of the Cocos finch is that they display an intra-species variation in feeding habits. The variation in foraging behaviors are hypothesized to be possible by observational learning from other Cocos finches. These feeding specializations are independent of any morphological changes, sex and age differences, rather, they are derived from an intra-species variation found within the Cocos finch. Still, despite its variation in feeding patterns, Cocos finches are categorized as one species with no known subspecies.


Description
The Cocos finch is about 12 cm (4.7 in) long and weighs about 12.5 g (0.44 oz). The bird has a black decurved pointed bill for eating berries and insects that are its main diet. They are sexually dimorphic in that the males are almost entirely black, having black feathers from the tail, breast, nape, and crown. Unlike male finches found on the Galapágos islands, male Cocos finches have black beaks year-round, never changing color.
Female Cocos finches have a lighter brown complexion compared to males. There is a black splattering of feathers on their breast area down to the flank and rump. The belly of the female Cocos finch is milky white compared to the mantle of the bird. The nape and crown area are more noticeably black. The young are similar, but have yellow bills.
The call of a Cocos finch can be described as a "buzzy" like sound with a high-pitched ending note. The beginning of the call can form a "djirr" sound followed by a high pitched "tiew" sound. Calls can also include a high pitched "phzzzz" sound.


Distribution and habitat

The Cocos finch is exclusively found in Cocos Island (which takes up a 1,997 km2 area), 580 km southeast from Costa Rica. Cocos finches are not known to have traveled outside of Cocos Island, making its habitat and reproduction range to only be 30 km2 (12 sq mi) of the entire island. The habitat of the Cocos finch is mostly within hibiscus thickets, closed-canopy forest, and woodland forest.
The climate within Cocos Island is described to be a typical rainforest climate with high humidity and chances for rainfall. The island has a varied landscape with bodies of lakes, streams, and caves and is home to other species including endemic lizards (only two species) and some migratory birds.


Ecology and behavior


Foraging patterns
Cocos finches are known to be a generalist species, although they are also known to specialize in an individual level which can result in different foraging behaviors within individual Cocos finches. This specialized behavior of Cocos finches can be explained through learning foraging patterns through other Cocos finches and can resonate said individual behavior to other Cocos Finches.


Breeding
Cocos finch usually nest throughout the year; however, their breeding season falls usually at around January–February. They form a nest using lichens and dry moss as their main material. The standard clutch is two brown-spotted white eggs, which are hatched in a roughly spherical nest built at the end of a tree branch. Eggs are in clutch sizes of about 4–5 with distinctive pink/light brown spots.


Threats
Cocos finches face invasive mammalian predators, like rats and cats, on the island. Despite these predators, there is no evidence of high predatory pressures on the Cocos finches themselves. Scientists conclude that due to the low predatory pressures on Cocos finches, they have developed this specialized feeding behavior to evolve to a more generalist species.


Status
Cocos Island is a natural reserved island, such that there is limited human interaction with the Cocos finch and other Cocos Island wildlife. While there has been increasing tourism around the island, there is no evidence of high disturbance rates from these tourists to affect the wildlife in the island including the Cocos finch.
Despite having a small distribution, due to the predation and disturbance rate of Cocos finches being low, along with a steady population rate, the IUCN deems the Cocos finch to be Least Concern.
Currently, there are estimated to be about 6,000–15,000 Cocos finches on Cocos Island.


References

Stiles, F. G.; Skutch, A. F. (October 1989). A guide to the birds of Costa Rica. Ithaca, NY: Cornell University Press. ISBN 0-8014-9600-4.


External links
BirdLife Species Factsheet.
Bird-Stamps.org's page, illustrations of two postage stamps containing images of the Cocos Island finch

[[File:Cocornis agassizi = Pinaroloxias inornata, cocos finch.jpg|thumb]]The '''Cocos finch''' ('''''Pinaroloxias inornata''''') or '''Cocos Island finch''', is the only one of [[Darwin's finches]] not native to the [[Galápagos Islands]], and the [[monotypic|only member]] of the [[genus]] '''''Pinaroloxias'''''. Sometimes classified in the family [[Emberizidae]], more recent studies have shown it to belong in the [[tanager]] family, [[Thraupidae]]. It is [[endemic (ecology)|endemic]] to [[Cocos Island]], a Pacific island which is approximately {{Convert|360|mi|km|abbr=}} south of [[Costa Rica]].

== Taxonomy ==
The Cocos finch was [[species description|formally described]] in 1843 by the English ornithologist [[John Gould]] under the [[binomial name]] ''Cactornis inornatus''.<ref>{{ cite book | last=Gould | first=John | author-link=John Gould | year=1844 | title=The Zoology of the Voyage of H.M.S. Sulphur : under the command of Captain Sir Edward Belcher, during the years 1836-42 | location=London | publisher=Smith, Elder | page=42, [https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/page/40020099 Plate 25] | url=https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/page/40020093 }} For the publication date see: {{ cite book | last=Zimmer | first=John T. | author-link=John T. Zimmer | year=1926 | title=Catalogue of the Edward E. Ayer Ornithological Library | series=Field Museum of Natural History, Zoology Series. Volume 16, Part 1 | volume=16 | location=Chicago | publisher=Field Museum of Natural History | page=304 | url=https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/page/36194957 | ref=none}}.</ref> The species was moved to a new genus ''Pinaroloxias'' by [[Richard Bowdler Sharpe]] in 1885.<ref>{{ cite book | last1=Sharpe | first1=R. Bowdler | authorlink1=Richard Bowdler Sharpe | year=1885 | title=Catalogue of the Passeriformes or Perching Birds in the Collection of the British Museum. Fringilliformes Part I | volume=10 | publisher=Trustees of the British Museum |location=London | page=52 | series=Catalogue of the Birds in the British Museum | url=https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/page/8324472 }}</ref> The genus name combines the [[Ancient Greek]] {{Lang|grc-Latn|pinaros}} meaning "dirty" or "squalid" with ''[[Loxia]]'', a genus introduced by [[Carl Linnaeus]] in 1758 for the crossbills. The specific epithet ''inornata'' is [[Latin]] for "plain" or "unadorned".<ref>{{cite book | last=Jobling | first=James A. | year=2010| title=The Helm Dictionary of Scientific Bird Names | publisher=Christopher Helm | location=London | isbn=978-1-4081-2501-4 | pages=205, 307 }}</ref> The Cocos finch is a member of a group collectively known as Darwin's finches.<ref>{{Cite journal | last1=Sato | first1=A. | last2=Tichy | first2=H. | last3=O'hUigin | first3=C. | last4=Grant | first4=P.R. | last5=Grant | first5=B.R. | last6=Klein | first6=J. | date=2001 | title=On the origin of Darwin's Finches | journal=Molecular Biology and Evolution | volume=18 | issue=3 | pages=299–311 | doi=10.1093/oxfordjournals.molbev.a003806 | pmid=11230531 | doi-access=free }}</ref> Although traditionally placed with the buntings and [[New World sparrow]]s in the family [[Emberizidae]],<ref>{{ cite book | editor-last=Paynter | editor-first=Raymond A. Jr | year=1970 | title=Check-List of Birds of the World | volume=13 | publisher=Museum of Comparative Zoology | place=Cambridge, Massachusetts | page=168 | url=https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/page/14483403 }}</ref> [[molecular phylogenetic]] studies have shown that Darwin's finches are members of the [[subfamily]] Coerebinae within the tanager family Thraupidae.<ref>{{cite journal| last1=Burns | first1=K.J. | last2=Shultz | first2=A.J. | last3=Title | first3=P.O. | last4=Mason | first4=N.A. | last5=Barker | first5=F.K. | last6=Klicka | first6=J. | last7=Lanyon | first7=S.M. | last8=Lovette | first8=I.J. | year=2014 | title=Phylogenetics and diversification of tanagers (Passeriformes: Thraupidae), the largest radiation of Neotropical songbirds | journal=Molecular Phylogenetics and Evolution| volume=75| pages=41–77 | doi=10.1016/j.ympev.2014.02.006 | pmid=24583021 | url=https://digitalcommons.lsu.edu/cgi/viewcontent.cgi?article=3613&context=biosci_pubs | url-access=subscription }}</ref> The Cocos finch was first categorized to be closer to the [[Warbler-finch|warbler finch]] [[clade]] due to the similar [[Morphology (biology)|morphology]] of cousin warbler finches.<ref name=":9">{{Cite journal|last1=Grant|first1=Peter|last2=Grant|first2=B. Rosemary|date=2002|title=Adaptive Radiation of Darwin's Finches|journal=American Scientist|volume=90|issue=2|pages=130|doi=10.1511/2002.2.130|issn=0003-0996|bibcode=2002AmSci..90..130G}}</ref><ref name=":10">{{Cite journal|last=Schluter|first=Dolph|date=September 1984|title=Morphological and Phylogenetic Relations Among the Darwin's Finches|journal=Evolution|volume=38|issue=5|pages=921–930|doi=10.2307/2408428|jstor=2408428|pmid=28555805}}</ref>

The Cocos finch shares many morphological similarities to other Darwin's finches despite having been separated from the Galapagos Islands.<ref name=":2">{{Cite journal|last1=Werner|first1=T. K.|last2=Sherry|first2=T. W.|date=1987-08-01|title=Behavioral feeding specialization in ''Pinaroloxias inornata'', the "Darwin's Finch" of Cocos Island, Costa Rica|journal=Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences|volume=84|issue=15|pages=5506–5510|doi=10.1073/pnas.84.15.5506|pmid=16593864|pmc=298888|issn=0027-8424|bibcode=1987PNAS...84.5506W|doi-access=free}}</ref> What is unique, however, of the Cocos finch is that they display an intra-species variation in feeding habits. The variation in foraging behaviors are hypothesized to be possible by [[observational learning]] from other Cocos finches.<ref name=":2" /><ref name=":3">{{Cite journal|last1=Smith|first1=James N. M.|last2=Sweatman|first2=Hugh P. A.|date=1976|title=Feeding habits and morphological variation in Cocos Finches|journal=The Condor | volume=78| issue=2| pages=244| doi=10.2307/1366860| jstor=1366860}}</ref> These feeding specializations are independent of any morphological changes, sex and age differences, rather, they are derived from an intra-species variation found within the Cocos finch. Still, despite its variation in feeding patterns, Cocos finches are categorized as one species with no known subspecies.<ref name=":3" />

== Description ==
The Cocos finch is about {{Convert|12|cm|abbr=on}} long and weighs about {{Convert|12.5|g|abbr=on}}. The bird has a black decurved pointed bill for eating berries and insects that are its main diet.<ref name=":2" /><ref name=":4">{{Cite web|title=Cocos Finch ''Pinaroloxias inornata''|url=http://datazone.birdlife.org/species/factsheet/cocos-finch-pinaroloxias-inornata/text|publisher=Bird Life International|website=Data Zone|access-date=2019-12-05|url-status=live|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20201020233729/http://datazone.birdlife.org/species/factsheet/cocos-finch-pinaroloxias-inornata/text |archive-date=2020-10-20 }}</ref><ref name=":5">{{Cite journal|last=Grant|first=Peter R.|date=March 2002|title=Birds, Mammals, & Reptiles of the Galápagos Islands: An Identification Guide. By Andy Swash and, Rob Still; with illustrations by Ian Lewington. New Haven (Connecticut): Yale University Press. $24.95. 168 p; ill.; index of English and scientific names. 2000.|url=https://archive.org/details/birdsmammalsrept00andy/page/79|journal=The Quarterly Review of Biology|volume=77|issue=1|pages=[https://archive.org/details/birdsmammalsrept00andy/page/79 79–79]|doi=10.1086/343641|issn=0033-5770|isbn=0-300-08864-7}}</ref> They are [[Sexual dimorphism|sexually dimorphic]] in that the males are almost entirely black, having black feathers from the tail, breast, nape, and [[Crown (anatomy)|crown]]. Unlike male finches found on the [[Galápagos Islands|Galapágos]] islands, male Cocos finches have black beaks year-round, never changing color.<ref name=":4" /><ref name=":6">{{Cite journal|last=Hudon|first=Jocelyn|date=January 1987|title=Guide des passereaux granivores. Embérizinés Gilbert C. Armani|journal=The Auk|volume=104|issue=1|pages=152–153|doi=10.2307/4087255|issn=0004-8038|jstor=4087255}}</ref><ref name=":5" />

Female Cocos finches have a lighter brown complexion compared to males. There is a black splattering of feathers on their breast area down to the flank and rump. The belly of the female Cocos finch is milky white compared to the [[Bird anatomy|mantle]] of the bird. The nape and crown area are more noticeably black.<ref name=":4" /><ref name=":1">{{Cite web|url=https://avibase.bsc-eoc.org/species.jsp?lang=EN&avibaseid=82F77F7B5D72DDBA&sec=itis|title=Cocos Island Finch ''Pinaroloxias inornata'' (Gould, 1843)|website=Avibase - The World Bird Database|access-date=2019-12-04}}</ref><ref name=":5" /> The young are similar, but have yellow [[beak|bills]].

The call of a Cocos finch can be described as a "buzzy" like sound with a high-pitched ending note. The beginning of the call can form a "djirr" sound followed by a high pitched "tiew" sound. Calls can also include a high pitched "phzzzz" sound.<ref name=":5" /><ref name=":4" />

== Distribution and habitat ==
[[File:Cocos Island.jpg|thumb|311x311px|Cocos Island viewed from a helicopter]]
The Cocos finch is exclusively found in Cocos Island (which takes up a 1,997 km<sup>2</sup> area), 580 km southeast from Costa Rica.<ref name=":4" /><ref name=":7">{{Citation|chapter=Cocos Island National Park, Costa Rica|date=2019-11-11|pages=91–92|publisher=Springer Singapore|isbn=978-981-13-2537-3|doi=10.1007/978-981-13-2538-0_368|title=Dictionary of Geotourism|s2cid=242743344 }}</ref> Cocos finches are not known to have traveled outside of Cocos Island, making its habitat and reproduction range to only be {{Convert|30|km2|abbr=on}} of the entire island.<ref name=":4" /> The habitat of the Cocos finch is mostly within [[Hibiscus|hibiscus thickets]], [[Canopy (biology)|closed-canopy forest]], and [[Woodland|woodland forest]].<ref name=":4" /><ref name=":5" />

The climate within Cocos Island is described to be a typical [[rainforest]] climate with high humidity and chances for rainfall. The island has a varied landscape with bodies of lakes, streams, and caves and is home to other species including endemic lizards (only two species) and some migratory birds.<ref name=":7" />

== Ecology and behavior ==

=== Foraging patterns ===
Cocos finches are known to be a [[Generalist and specialist species|generalist species]], although they are also known to specialize in an individual level which can result in different foraging behaviors within individual Cocos finches.<ref name=":2" /> This specialized behavior of Cocos finches can be explained through learning foraging patterns through other Cocos finches and can resonate said individual behavior to other Cocos Finches.<ref name=":2" /><ref name=":3" />

=== Breeding ===
Cocos finch usually nest throughout the year; however, their breeding season falls usually at around January–February.<ref name=":7" /><ref name=":11">{{Cite journal|last=Raffaele|first=Herbert A.|date=September 1990|title=A Guide to the Birds of Costa Rica.F. Gary Stiles, Alexander F. Skutch|journal=The Quarterly Review of Biology|volume=65|issue=3|pages=365–366|doi=10.1086/416883|issn=0033-5770}}</ref> They form a nest using lichens and dry moss as their main material. The standard clutch is two brown-spotted white [[bird egg|eggs]], which are hatched in a roughly spherical [[bird nest|nest]] built at the end of a tree branch. Eggs are in clutch sizes of about 4–5 with distinctive pink/light brown spots.<ref name=":5" /><ref name=":6" />

=== Threats ===
Cocos finches face invasive mammalian predators, like rats and cats, on the island. Despite these predators, there is no evidence of high predatory pressures on the Cocos finches themselves. Scientists conclude that due to the low predatory pressures on Cocos finches, they have developed this specialized feeding behavior to evolve to a more generalist species.<ref name=":3" /><ref name=":4" />

== Status ==
Cocos Island is a natural reserved island, such that there is limited human interaction with the Cocos finch and other Cocos Island wildlife. While there has been increasing tourism around the island, there is no evidence of high [[Disturbance (ecology)|disturbance]] rates from these tourists to affect the wildlife in the island including the Cocos finch.<ref name=":4" /><ref name=":8">{{Cite journal|last1=Butchart|first1=Stuart H M|last2=Stattersfield|first2=Alison J|last3=Bennun|first3=Leon A|last4=Shutes|first4=Sue M|last5=Akçakaya|first5=H Resit|last6=Baillie|first6=Jonathan E M|last7=Stuart|first7=Simon N|last8=Hilton-Taylor|first8=Craig|last9=Mace|first9=Georgina M|date=2004-10-26|editor-last=Walt V Reid|title=Measuring Global Trends in the Status of Biodiversity: Red List Indices for Birds|journal=PLOS Biology|language=en|volume=2|issue=12|pages=e383|doi=10.1371/journal.pbio.0020383|issn=1545-7885|pmc=524254|pmid=15510230 |doi-access=free }}</ref>

Despite having a small distribution, due to the [[predation]] and disturbance rate of Cocos finches being low, along with a steady population rate, the [[International Union for Conservation of Nature|IUCN]] deems the Cocos finch to be Least Concern.<ref name=":12" /><ref name=":8" />

Currently, there are estimated to be about 6,000–15,000 Cocos finches on Cocos Island.<ref name=IUCN/><ref name=":8" /><ref name=":12">{{Cite book|date=1996-10-01|title=Birds to watch 2: the world list of threatened birds: the official source for birds on the IUCN red list|pages=34–0930c-34–0930c}}</ref>

zh.extract:
可岛雀（台湾作可可斯島雀，学名：Pinaroloxias inornata），是雀形目唐纳雀科的一种鸟类，是可岛雀属（学名：Pinaroloxias）的唯一物种。
可岛雀体重约13.1克，翼长约62.2毫米，嘴峰长约14.5毫米，喙宽度约4.2毫米，喙厚度约5.5毫米，跗蹠长约20.6毫米，尾长约40.2毫米。可岛雀在其分布区内为留鸟，其栖息在半开放的高大树木组成的森林中，食性为杂食性。
可岛雀分布于哥斯达黎加西部可可斯島沿海地带的森林。该物种是单型物种，没有亚种分化。
